rejectionReason: We were unable to review your app because no demo account credentials were provided.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted an app that requires login to access core functionality but did not provide demo account credentials in the App Review Information section of App Store Connect. The reviewer could not sign in and therefore could not review the app.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: We are unable to complete the review of your app because we need a demo account to fully access your app's features. Please provide demo account credentials (username and password) in the App Review Information section of App Store Connect.
fix: Created a dedicated demo/review account with full access to all features. Added the username and password in App Store Connect > App Information > App Review Information > Sign-in required. Included notes describing which features to test and any special setup instructions.

rejectionReason: The demo account requires SMS-based two-factor authentication that the reviewer cannot complete.
whatDeveloperDid: Provided demo account credentials but the account required SMS-based 2FA. The verification code was sent to the developer's phone number, and the Apple reviewer in California had no way to receive or enter the code.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: We were unable to log in to your app with the provided demo credentials. The account requires SMS verification and we are unable to receive the verification code. Please provide a demo account that can be fully accessed without additional verification steps or provide a way for us to complete the verification.
fix: Created a review-specific account with 2FA disabled. Alternatively, if 2FA cannot be disabled: set up a shared email account where the reviewer can receive verification codes, or use TOTP-based 2FA and share the TOTP secret/QR code in the review notes so the reviewer can generate codes themselves.

rejectionReason: Your support URL does not provide a way for users to contact you.
whatDeveloperDid: Provided a support URL in App Store Connect that linked to a generic company homepage, a blank page, or a page without any contact form, email address, phone number, or other way for users to get support.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.3 - Performance - Accurate Metadata: The support URL you provided does not include any contact information or mechanism for users to reach you for support. The support URL must provide a way for users to contact you with questions or issues about your app.
fix: Created a dedicated support page with a contact form, support email address, and FAQ section. The page is accessible without login and provides clear ways for users to report issues and get help. Updated the support URL in App Store Connect.
behavioralSignals: no contact form on support page

rejectionReason: We need a video demonstration to understand how your app works.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted an app with hardware-dependent features (Bluetooth device pairing, AR experiences, IoT integration, or features requiring specific real-world conditions) that the Apple reviewer could not test in their office environment.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: We are unable to fully evaluate your app's functionality because it requires specific hardware or real-world conditions we cannot replicate. Please provide a video that demonstrates the app's complete functionality, including the features that require external hardware or specific conditions.
fix: Recorded a clear screen recording (or combination of screen + physical demo) showing the app's full functionality. Uploaded the video to an accessible URL (YouTube unlisted, Dropbox, etc.) and included the link in the App Review notes. The video showed: device pairing, feature usage, and all key user flows.

rejectionReason: Your macOS app's Full Disk Access permission is not displaying correctly in System Preferences.
whatDeveloperDid: Built a macOS app that required Full Disk Access for backup or file management functionality. Due to a macOS bug, the app's entry in System Preferences > Privacy & Security > Full Disk Access was not showing the correct app name or icon, making it impossible for the reviewer to grant the permission.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: We were unable to grant Full Disk Access to your app. The app does not appear correctly in the Full Disk Access section of System Preferences. Without this permission, we cannot test the app's core functionality.
fix: Ensured the app is properly code-signed and notarized. Added the correct bundle identifier and app name in Info.plist. For the macOS bug: provided detailed instructions in the review notes on how to manually add the app to Full Disk Access via the '+' button. Filed a Feedback with Apple about the System Preferences display issue.
behavioralSignals: app not showing in Full Disk Access

rejectionReason: Your previously approved app has been removed from the App Store and re-rejected upon resubmission.
whatDeveloperDid: Doppel AI was initially approved and published on the App Store. It was later removed by Apple for guideline concerns (potentially related to deepfake/AI-generated content of real people). When the developer attempted to resubmit with modifications, the app was rejected again with additional scrutiny.
whatAppleSaid: Your app was previously removed from the App Store for guideline violations. Upon re-review, we have determined that the app still does not comply with the App Store Review Guidelines. Your app creates AI-generated likenesses of real people, which raises concerns under Guidelines 1.1 and 5.1 regarding user safety and privacy.
fix: The developer had to fundamentally rethink the app's approach to AI-generated content. Added consent verification for any real person whose likeness is generated, removed the ability to create content of public figures without authorization, implemented content safety filters, and added clear labeling of all AI-generated content.

rejectionReason: Your app's deep link handling is broken, causing incorrect navigation.
whatDeveloperDid: The app's Universal Links and deep link handling broke after an iOS 18 update. Deep links from emails and web pages either opened Safari instead of the app, opened the app to the wrong screen, or caused a redirect loop between the app and Safari.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: We found that deep links to your app do not work correctly. Tapping a link that should open your app instead opens Safari, or opens the app to an incorrect screen. Please ensure your app's deep link handling works correctly on iOS 18.
fix: Updated the Apple App Site Association (AASA) file to use the new format compatible with iOS 18. Verified the AASA file is served with the correct Content-Type (application/json). Updated the app's URL handling code to use the new UIScene-based URL handling. Tested all deep link scenarios on iOS 18 devices.
behavioralSignals: deep links open Safari instead of app
redirect loop between app and Safari

rejectionReason: Your cryptocurrency wallet app does not comply with regulatory requirements for the regions where it operates.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted a cryptocurrency wallet app that allowed users to buy, sell, and trade cryptocurrencies but did not have the required financial services licenses or registrations for the jurisdictions where it planned to operate.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 3.1.5(b) - Business - Cryptocurrencies: Your app facilitates cryptocurrency transactions but does not appear to comply with applicable legal requirements. Cryptocurrency exchanges and wallets must be offered by enrolled developers that are licensed or registered to provide such services in the jurisdictions where the app is available.
fix: Restricted the app's availability to jurisdictions where the company holds valid licenses. Provided Apple with documentation of regulatory compliance (money transmitter licenses, FinCEN registration, etc.). Added required legal disclosures, terms of service, and risk warnings within the app.

rejectionReason: Your app bundle exceeds the maximum allowed size.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted an app whose total bundle size (including all architectures and resources) exceeded 4GB. The app included large uncompressed assets, multiple video files, and unoptimized images that pushed the binary size over Apple's limit.
whatAppleSaid: ERROR ITMS-90381: The app's total uncompressed size exceeds the maximum allowed size of 4GB. Your app bundle (including all architectures) is approximately 4.7GB. Please reduce the size of your app.
fix: Used On-Demand Resources (ODR) to host large assets on Apple's servers and download them only when needed. Compressed image and video assets. Used Asset Catalogs with app slicing to deliver only the assets needed for each device. Moved large content to a post-install download with user consent.

rejectionReason: Your app requires users to accept push notifications to use the app.
whatDeveloperDid: Displayed the push notification permission dialog on launch and, if the user denied it, showed a blocking screen that prevented access to the app until notifications were enabled. The app essentially held core functionality hostage to push notification consent.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 5.1.1 - Legal - Privacy: Your app requires users to grant push notification permission to access the app's functionality. Users must be able to use your app's core features without being required to accept push notifications. Permission requests must be optional and the app must remain functional if the user declines.
fix: Made push notification permission optional. Removed the blocking screen. The app now functions fully without push notifications enabled. Added a gentle, non-blocking prompt explaining the benefits of notifications at a contextually appropriate moment (e.g., after booking an appointment: 'Would you like to receive reminders?').
behavioralSignals: can't use app without push enabled

rejectionReason: Your app requests 'Always' location permission when 'While Using' would be sufficient.
whatDeveloperDid: Requested 'Always' location permission (requestAlwaysAuthorization) for a restaurant finder app that only needed location while the user was actively browsing. Background location was not needed for any user-facing feature.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 5.1.2 - Legal - Privacy - Data Use and Sharing: Your app requests Always location authorization, but your app's use of location appears to only require 'While Using the App' authorization. Please request the minimum level of location access necessary for your app's functionality.
fix: Changed from requestAlwaysAuthorization() to requestWhenInUseAuthorization(). Updated NSLocationWhenInUseUsageDescription with a clear description. Removed NSLocationAlwaysAndWhenInUseUsageDescription and NSLocationAlwaysUsageDescription from Info.plist since background location is not needed.

rejectionReason: Your app's location permission flow does not account for the iOS 13+ provisional 'Allow Once' behavior.
whatDeveloperDid: The app requested Always location permission, but starting with iOS 13, the system no longer shows 'Always Allow' as an option in the initial prompt. Instead, iOS shows 'Allow While Using App', 'Allow Once', and 'Don't Allow'. The 'Always' option only appears later as a separate system prompt if the app demonstrates legitimate background location usage.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 5.1.2 - Legal - Privacy - Data Use and Sharing: Your app's location permission handling does not properly adapt to iOS 13+ behavior. The app expects Always location authorization but the initial system prompt no longer offers this option. Please update your app to gracefully handle the provisional 'While Using' permission and request escalation to Always only when justified.
fix: Updated the permission flow to first request When In Use authorization, then request Always authorization as a separate step. Handled the CLAuthorizationStatus.authorizedWhenInUse state gracefully. The app now works with When In Use and only prompts for escalation to Always when the user triggers a feature that genuinely requires background location.
behavioralSignals: doesn't handle When In Use fallback

rejectionReason: Your app's metadata language does not match the primary language of the app.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted an app where the App Store metadata (title, subtitle, description, keywords) was in English, but the app's UI was entirely in Korean. Or vice versa: app was in English but metadata was submitted in another language's storefront.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.3 - Performance - Accurate Metadata: Your app's App Store metadata language does not match the primary language of your app. The app description and title are in English, but the app's interface is in Korean. Please ensure your metadata language matches your app's primary language for each localization.
fix: Matched the App Store metadata language to the app's primary language for each localization. Added proper localized metadata for each language the app supports. Used App Store Connect's localization feature to provide language-specific metadata for each storefront.
behavioralSignals: metadata in different language than app

rejectionReason: Your app's release notes contain irrelevant or inappropriate content.
whatDeveloperDid: Used the release notes field for promotional messaging ('SALE: 50% off premium!'), internal development notes ('Fixed Bob's bug from Sprint 23'), social media links, or unrelated commentary instead of describing what changed in the update.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.3 - Performance - Accurate Metadata: Your app's release notes contain content that is not relevant to the app update. Release notes should describe what is new or changed in the current version. Please update your release notes to accurately describe the changes in this update.
fix: Rewrote release notes to focus on user-facing changes: new features, bug fixes, and improvements. Removed promotional content, internal references, and unrelated commentary. Example: 'What's New: - Fixed an issue where photos wouldn't save correctly. - Improved loading speed for the feed. - Added dark mode support.'

rejectionReason: Your app's metadata contains emoji characters which are not permitted in certain fields.
whatDeveloperDid: Used emoji characters in the app name, subtitle, or description in App Store Connect. While some apps have emojis in their metadata, Apple's enforcement is inconsistent - some apps get rejected while similar apps with emojis remain published.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.3 - Performance - Accurate Metadata: Your app's name/subtitle includes emoji characters. App names and subtitles should use only standard text characters. Please remove emoji from your app name and subtitle. Note: emoji may be acceptable in the app description in some cases.
fix: Removed all emoji characters from the app name and subtitle fields. Emojis can sometimes be used in the longer description field, but it's safest to avoid them entirely. Used descriptive text instead of emoji to convey the app's purpose.
